title: Addiction and Social Anxiety
author: Ned

seo.title: The Intersection of Addiction and Social Anxiety: An In-Depth Analysis
seo.description: Discover the complex relationship between social anxiety and addiction. Understand how these two conditions intertwine, leading to a cyclical pattern of substance use and anxiety, and explore evidence-based therapeutic interventions.

body:
We all live with a certain amount of social inhibition. We don’t want to do something that causes people to judge us or laugh at us. We are social beings -- for our ancestors, ostracization from the tribe meant certain death. Though being laughed at in public is quite different than being thrown out of the tribe, we still carry the knowledge that embarrassing ourselves or being awkward around strangers is undesirable. 

People with social anxiety live with heightened perceptions of danger in social situations. They feel that committing a minor faux-pas will cause them to be thrown out of the tribe. To a person with social anxiety, doing something that causes another person to laugh at them feels akin to a physical threat.

A person without social anxiety might feel uncomfortable doing something that involves a social risk. For example, they might feel awkward asking an attractive stranger out on a date.

Despite this discomfort, they are able to accurately perceive the pros and cons of this semi-risky behavior:

  • If the stranger says yes, it could mean the beginning of a high-value relationship.
  • If the stranger says no, it may be awkward and disappointing, but will be a tolerable experience.

If the answer is no, they might cringe thinking about the experience later. But they still continue on with social life as usual.

To the person with social anxiety, the task of asking a stranger on a date feels herculean. They have thoughts such as:

  • “This stranger will say no and everyone will laugh and me”.

  • “This person will think I’m weird and tell all their friends how weird I am.”

  • "I'm so weird around people, they'll definitely say no and I'll look like an idiot."

  • “This will feel so awful and make me so anxious and terrible, I shouldn’t do it.”

  • "If I do this I'll blush and stutter and everyone will see and I'll be so humiliated."

  • "I'll never forget how terrible I feel if this goes wrong."

Individuals with social anxiety percieve important parts of life, like dating and finding a partner, as acutely dangerous and frightening.

The individual with social anxiety sees social situations as being far more dangerous than they actually are. Thus, they’re more likely to stay home, avoid strangers and all possible social conflicts, and not live the life that they want. 

How do addiction and social anxiety interact?

According to the National Comorbidity Study (NCS),  people with social anxiety are:

  • 3.3 times more likely to develop an alcohol use disorder
  • 2.8 times more likely to develop an illicit substance use disorder

(both compared to people who do not have social anxiety.)

Substances sometimes feel like a miracle cure to individuals with social anxiety. They may find that parts of their personality come out that they’ve never seen before. Suddenly, their quiet social persona acts in a way that feels charming and talkative. They may find engaging in social situations easy and fun. 

Many people with social anxiety think that the drugs are adding something to their personality, changing who they are. They believe that they aren’t innately fun or enjoyable to be around -- it’s the substance that makes them interesting and engaging. And in an effort to increase the effects, they consume even more of the substance. The logic is simple: if I feel this good when I’ve had two beers, then I’ll feel twice as good if I’ve had four beers.

Of course, intoxication does not work this way. While a socially anxious person may feel very little anxiety when heavily intoxicated, they may actually be making their worst fears come true. They may unskillfully bumble through interactions. They may frustrate friends and loved ones with their need for babysitting. All the while, they remain blissfully unaware, lost in intoxication.

Individuals with social anxiety sometimes use illicit substances as a crutch to get them through social situations.

The socially anxious individual may also attempt to use the “miracle cure” of substance use across all social situations. They might start by drinking heavily at social gatherings, and then begin drinking in class and at work as well. Eventually, substance use pervades every domain of their life in which they interact with other people. They lose the ability to operate independent of their substance of choice, even when alone.

How can social anxiety and addiction be treated?

It helps to first treat the root of the substance use problem, which is social anxiety. This can be done through a combination of cognitive and exposure therapy.

In cognitive therapy, the therapist can help the person recognize times when their social anxiety had been triggered, and analyze if the situation really deserved such fear. People with social anxiety often think that people think and focus on them much more than they actually are. Cognitive therapy helps socially anxious people learn that the spotlight isn’t always on them.

The exposure part of the therapy involves engaging in what the client says that they normally avoid to help them learn that this behavior is actually safe.

For example, people with social anxiety often text instead of talk on the phone. To treat this behavior, the therapist would begin by exploring which types of phone calls make them the most anxious. For example, the client might state that they are able to call their mother will little to no anxiety. They experience moderate anxiety when speaking to a relatively new friend on the phone, and great anxiety when speaking to a stranger. The therapist would then work with them to engage initially in the easy phone call with their mother. They'd then move on to the harder calls in order of difficulty. This way, the person can learn along the way that their feared stimulus was not as terrible and intolerable as they expected.
